summary: Requires SMB signing for inbound server connections by setting both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under LanmanServer. Microsoft directly documents the same registry key, value names, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for this SMB server signing posture.

setting.name: SMB Server Signing Requirement
setting.casual_explanation: SMB signing helps Windows verify that file-sharing traffic was not tampered with. Requiring it on the server side makes the machine stricter about inbound SMB connections.
setting.why_people_change_it: People require SMB server signing to harden shared folders and reduce the chance of tampered SMB traffic. They relax it only when old clients or appliances still need weaker behavior.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Requiring SMB signing is safer for hosted shares, but some old clients may fail to connect, and there can be a performance cost on weaker hardware.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents both RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature under HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. The current app sets both to 1, which matches the stricter SMB server signing posture.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows determines the SMB server signing requirement based on its built-in release baseline.

allowed_values.label: Signing not required
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB server does not require signing for inbound connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing required
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB server requires signing for inbound connections.

allowed_values.label: Windows managed baseline
allowed_values.meaning: Windows determines whether SMB server signing is enabled for compatible connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing not enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB server does not enable signing for normal negotiated connections.

allowed_values.label: Signing enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMB server enables signing for compatible inbound connections.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1, matching the documented stricter SMB server signing posture.

windows_defaults.label: Version-dependent Windows SMB server baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows SMB server releases with signing support

states.rationale: Microsoft changes SMB signing defaults across Windows releases and scenarios.
Microsoft changes SMB signing defaults across Windows releases and scenarios.

windows_defaults.notes: This record does not collapse all Windows SMB server defaults into one static numeric baseline.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows managed default
intended_for: Users who want the release baseline chosen by Windows
Systems that are not ready to force a server-side SMB signing override
avoid_for: Security baselines that explicitly require SMB signing on hosted shares

states.rationale: Leave the explicit server signing override absent.
Leave the explicit server signing override absent.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want Windows to keep its own SMB server baseline for the installed release.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a documented hard requirement for SMB signing on hosted shares.
tradeoffs: You avoid forcing a server override.
Behavior can differ across Windows versions and deployment scenarios.

recommended_profiles.label: Require signing
intended_for: People who want explicit integrity protection on hosted SMB shares

states.rationale: Require signing for inbound SMB server connections.
Keep SMB signing enabled for compatible inbound connections alongside the requirement bit.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the app to force the stricter SMB server signing posture.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You still accept old SMB clients that cannot handle signing correctly.
tradeoffs: Hosted SMB shares gain stronger integrity protection.
Some legacy SMB clients may fail or perform worse.

evidence.title: Microsoft SMB signing overview
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the LanmanServer RequireSecuritySignature and EnableSecuritySignature registry values and explains SMB signing defaults and behavior.

evidence.title: Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always)
evidence.summary: Official Microsoft security option guidance for the server-side always-sign SMB policy.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes RequireSecuritySignature = 1 and EnableSecuritySignature = 1 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManServer\Parameters -> RequireSecuritySignature and explains that SMB server signing is controlled through the same LanManServer runtime path the app writes. The same page also explains that for SMB2 and later, EnableSecuritySignature is ignored and signing is controlled by being required or not, so the app's extra EnableSecuritySignature write stays on the documented surface even if it is redundant for modern SMB.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: Microsoft network server: Digitally sign communications (always) Registry key: HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanManServer\Parameters Registry value: RequireSecuritySignature Data Type: REG_DWORD Data: 0 (disable), 1 (enable). The EnableSecuritySignature registry setting for SMB2 and later clients and servers is ignored. Therefore, this setting does nothing unless you're using SMB1.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents the exact LanManServer runtime path used by the app for RequireSecuritySignature and explicitly explains the modern SMB2+ semantics of EnableSecuritySignature on the same signing surface.
